text.title: Micro-MVP Experiment Types
text.summary: Six micro-MVP types with effort, what each validates, and the conditions under which each is the right choice.

rule.statement: Landing page (1 day): validates demand and messaging. Use when the core question is "do people want this?" Measure: signup or waitlist conversion rate against a pre-registered threshold.
rule.rationale: A landing page with a CTA isolates demand signal from product quality signal. The Dropbox 3-minute video generated a 75K waitlist from 5K overnight at cost of video production time only.

rule.statement: Concierge (1 week): validates workflow fit and value delivery. Use when the question is "does this process actually help users?" A human manually delivers the service. Measure: task completion rate, willingness to return.
rule.rationale: Concierge MVPs reveal process failures before automating them. Automating a broken workflow is more expensive than discovering it is broken during manual delivery.

rule.statement: Wizard of Oz (days): validates UX and willingness to pay. The user sees a polished interface; humans operate the backend manually. Limit to approximately 50 concurrent users before manual operation collapses.
rule.rationale: Decouples UX validation from automation cost. Answers "would users pay for this experience?" without building the engine.

rule.statement: Video demo (hours): validates interest and virality. Use for consumer products where the value is immediately demonstrable. Measure: shares, comment sentiment, waitlist signups.
rule.rationale: Video is the cheapest signal for "does this concept resonate?" not "does this work?" Do not use as a willingness-to-pay signal.

rule.statement: Fake door (hours): validates feature demand. Show a button or menu item for a non-existent feature. Measure: click-through rate. Requires ethical unwind: inform users the feature does not exist yet.
rule.rationale: Fake doors measure intent, not interest. A user who clicks "Export to CSV" intended to use it. Plan the ethical unwind before launch — users who discover the deception lose trust.

rule.statement: Smoke test / Stripe Payment Link (days): validates price sensitivity and willingness to pay. Show a pricing page with a real checkout. The only reliable willingness-to-pay signal is the credit-card moment.
rule.rationale: Self-reported willingness to pay (surveys) overstates actual behavior by 3-5x. A Stripe checkout with real money is the only honest signal.

example.note: Dropbox: assumed people wanted easy file sync. Micro-MVP: 3-minute video explaining the concept. Result: waitlist from 5K to 75K overnight. Cost: only video production time. Validated demand before writing a single line of sync code.
Running a concierge MVP beyond 50 concurrent users without automating anything. The team discovers it is now operating a service business, not validating a product hypothesis.
